papers.title: ARPA-H Pours $144M into Healthspan-Focused Human Trials
papers.summary: The US government's ARPA-H has committed up to $144M across seven research teams under its PROSPR program, treating aging as a tractable biological problem and funding trials designed to show results within 1-3 years using surrogate biomarkers rather than waiting decades for disease outcomes. Two big awards stand out: Cambrian Bio gets $30.8M for a next-gen oral rapamycin analog (selective mTORC1 inhibitor), and Linnaeus gets $22M to trial LNS8801 — a GPER-targeting drug already tested in 100+ cancer patients — for healthspan preservation. This is the clearest signal yet that geroscience is now a mainstream government funding priority, not a fringe bet.

papers.title: Boosting Mitochondrial Metabolism in Neurons Enhances Long-Term Memory
papers.summary: Published today in Nature Metabolism, researchers showed that expanding mitochondrial energy capacity in memory circuits directly improves long-term memory formation in both flies and mice — not just restoring it, but enhancing it beyond normal baselines. The trick was knocking down Letm1, a mitochondrial calcium exporter, causing neurons to retain more Ca2+ and run their metabolism harder on demand. The finding reframes the age-related cognitive decline story: it's not just neuron death or plaques, it's energy supply — and that's a much more tractable therapeutic target.

papers.title: Circular Mitochondrial RNAs Decline with Aging and Drive Cellular Senescence
papers.summary: A newly characterised class of RNA — circular RNAs generated from mitochondrial DNA — turns out to play a key role in keeping cells young. Specifically, circular MT-RNR2 supports TCA cycle activity and is significantly depleted in old humans and senescent fibroblasts. When the binding protein GRSF1 is knocked down, circMT-RNR2 drops, fumarate and succinate fall with it, and cells accelerate into senescence. This opens a new molecular handle on mitochondrial aging that has nothing to do with the usual ROS or mtDNA damage narratives.

papers.title: Partial Reprogramming Is Conserved from Insect to Mammal
papers.summary: A review in Cells consolidates the evidence that OSKM-based partial reprogramming extends lifespan in both fruit flies and mice — two evolutionarily distant species — suggesting the mechanism is deeply conserved and not an experimental artifact. The authors also link reprogramming to senolytic action, noting that differentiated cells retain unexpected plasticity throughout life that can be unlocked therapeutically. The convergence of aging clocks, senolytics, and reprogramming into a unified intervention framework is increasingly the consensus direction for the field.

papers.title: Resistance Exercise Slows Brain Aging in RCT of 309 Older Adults
papers.summary: A rigorous year-long RCT across 309 adults aged 62-70 used MRI-based brain clock models and found that both heavy and moderate resistance training measurably slow brain aging — not just at the hippocampus but globally across brain structures. The non-exercise control group showed continued age-related changes while both exercise arms held steady. Given that the only things that robustly move brain clocks in humans are either exercise or aggressive pharmacological interventions, this is a straightforward but well-evidenced data point worth revisiting.

papers.title: Senescent Cell Clearance Benefits Are Female-Specific in Mice
papers.summary: Using integrative omics across multiple tissues, this Adv Sci study found that clearing p16+ senescent cells in aged mice produces pronounced health and longevity benefits in females — but surprisingly muted effects in males. The mechanism appears tied to how sex hormones modulate the SASP and surrounding tissue response to senescent cell removal. This has direct implications for how senolytic trials are designed: sex-stratified analysis may be essential, and clinical trials that don't separate by sex may be masking real efficacy.

papers.title: TDP-43 Aggregation Identified as Driver of Vascular Dementia Progression
papers.summary: Researchers have found that TDP-43, a protein known to aggregate in ALS and FTD, also accumulates pathologically in vascular dementia — a condition caused by reduced blood flow to the brain rather than amyloid or tau. The finding expands TDP-43's role across neurodegenerative conditions and suggests the protein aggregation cascade shares common mechanisms regardless of the initial trigger. It adds another target to the growing list of clearable protein aggregates that may be contributing to age-related cognitive decline.
